---
format:
  pdf:
    geometry: "top=1.5cm, bottom=1.5cm, left=1.5cm, right=1.5cm"
    fontsize: 9pt
    papersize: letter
    toc: false
    colorlinks: true
    keep-tex: false
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \usepackage{longtable}
        \usepackage{array}
        \usepackage{fancyhdr}
        \pagestyle{fancy}
        \fancyhf{}
        \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0pt}
        \fancyfoot[C]{\small Python for R Users — JSM 2026 Workshop}
        \fancyfoot[R]{\small \thepage}
---


# Python for R Users — Quick Reference


\vspace{0.75em}


**JSM 2026 Workshop**

A side-by-side reference for R users transitioning to Python. Material created by Ethan Meyers (Ethan.Meyers@gmail.com)


\vspace{0.75em}


## 1. Syntax & Operators

| Concept |     R     |  Python  |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| Assignment | `x <- 3` or `x = 3` | `x = 3` |
| Exponentiation | `2^3` | `2**3` |
| Integer division | *(none)* | `17 // 5` → `3` |
| Modulo | `17 %% 5` | `17 % 5` |
| Logical AND | `&&` or `&` | `and` |
| Logical OR | `\|\|` or `\|` | `or` |
| Logical NOT | `!` | `not` |
| Equality | `==` | `==` |
| Inequality | `!=` | `!=` |
| Pipe | `\|>` or `%>%` | *(method chaining: `df.op1().op2()`)* |
| Code blocks | `{ }` | Indentation |
| Comments | `#` | `#` |
| Print | `cat()` / `print()` | `print()` (required in scripts) |

: {tbl-colwidths="[25,40,35]"}


> ⚠️ In Python, `^` is **bitwise XOR** — not exponentiation




$\newline$




## 2. Data Types

| Concept |     R     |  Python  |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| Integer | `3L` | `3` |
| Float | `3.14` | `3.14` |
| String | `"hello"` | `"hello"` |
| Boolean | `TRUE` / `FALSE` | `True` / `False` |
| Null | `NULL` | `None` |
| Missing | `NA` | *(no base equivalent; Pandas uses `NaN`)* |
| Check type | `class(x)` | `type(x)` |
| String interpolation | `glue::glue("The value is {my_var}")` | `f"The value is {my_var}"` |
| Check for NULL/None | `is.null(x)` | `x is None` |


$\pagebreak$



## 3. Data Structures

\vspace{0.5em}


### Python Lists $\leftrightarrow$ R Vectors 

| Operation | R (vector) | Python (list) |
|-----------|------------|---------------|
| Create | `c(1, 2, 3)`  | `[1, 2, 3]` |
| First element  | `v[1]` | `my_list[0]` ← **0-based!** |
| Last element  | `v[length(v)]` | `my_list[-1]` |
| Slice (2nd–4th)  | `v[2:4]` *(includes 4)* | `my_list[1:4]` *(excludes 4)* |
| Slice with step  | `v[seq(1, n, by=2)]` | `my_list[::2]` |
| Length | `length(v)`  | `len(my_list)` |
| Append | `c(v, x)`  | `my_list.append(x)` *(in place)* |
| Copy | *(copy-on-modify)*  | `my_list.copy()` ← required! |
| Vectorized math  | `v + 1` ✓ | `my_list + 1` ✗ → use NumPy |
| Named equivalent  | *(named vector)*  | dictionary `{}`  |

\vspace{0.5em}


### Python Dictionaries $\leftrightarrow$ R Named Lists 

| Operation |   R (named list)   |   Python (dict)   |
|-----------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Create | `my_list <- list(a=1, b=2)` | `d = {"a": 1, "b": 2}` |
| Access | `my_list$a`  or  `my_list[["a"]]` | `d["a"]` |
| Add/update | `my_list$c <- 3` | `d["c"] = 3` |
| Membership | `"a" %in% names(my_list)` | `"a" in d` |
| Safe access | returns `NULL` | `d.get("a", default)` |

: {tbl-colwidths="[25,40,35]"}


$\newline$



## 4. Control Flow

| Concept |     R     |  Python  |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| For loop | `for (x in v) { ... }` | `for x in my_list:` |
| Range of integers | `seq(0, n-1)` or `1:n` | `range(n)` → *0 to n - 1* |
| Index + value | `for (i in seq_along(v)) { x <- v[i] }` | `for i, x in enumerate(my_list):` |
| Parallel iteration | `mapply(f, v1, v2)` | `for a, b in zip(list1, list2):` |
| While loop | `while (cond) { ... }` | `while cond:` |
| If/else if/else | `if () {} else if () {} else {}` | `if:` / `elif:` / `else:` |
| Inline if | `ifelse(cond, a, b)` | `a if cond else b` |
| List transform | `sapply(v, f)` / `purrr::map(v, f)` | `[f(x) for x in my_list]` |
| Filter transform | `Filter(f, v)` / `purrr::keep(v, f)` | `[x for x in my_list if f(x)]` |



$\newline$



## 5. Functions

| Concept |     R     |  Python  |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| Define | `f <- function(x, y=0) { ... }` | `def f(x, y=0):` |
| Return | Last expression (implicit) | `return value` (explicit) |
| No return | `NULL` | `None` |
| Multiple return | Returns last value | Returns tuple; unpack with `a, b = f()` |
| Check missing arg | `missing(x)` | `if x is None:` |

> ⚠️ Never use a mutable object (list, dict) as a default argument. Use `None` and create inside the function.



$\pagebreak$



## 6. NumPy $\leftrightarrow$ R Vectors

| Concept |     R     |  Python  |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| Load package | *Part of base R* | `import numpy as np` |
| Create | `c(1, 2, 3)` | `np.array([1, 2, 3])` |
| Sequence | `seq(0, 10, by=2)` | `np.arange(0, 11, 2)` |
| Evenly spaced | `seq(0, 1, length.out=5)` | `np.linspace(0, 1, 5)` |
| Zeros | `rep(0, n)` | `np.zeros(n)` |
| Ones | `rep(1, n)` | `np.ones(n)` |
| Element-wise math | `v + 1`, `v * 2` | `arr + 1`, `arr * 2` |
| Math functions | `sqrt(v)`, `log(v)` | `np.sqrt(arr)`, `np.log(arr)` |
| Boolean filter | `v[v > 3]` | `arr[arr > 3]` |
| Combined filter | `v[v > 1 & v < 4]` | `arr[(arr > 1) & (arr < 4)]` |
| Mean / SD | `mean(v)`, `sd(v)` | `np.mean(arr)`, `np.std(arr)` |
| Mean, NAs removed | `mean(v, na.rm=TRUE)` | `np.nanmean(arr)` |
| Vectorized conditional | `ifelse(v > 3, "hi", "lo")` | `np.where(arr > 3, "hi", "lo")` |
| Set a seed | `set.seed(42)` | `rng = np.random.default_rng(42)` |
| Normal draws | `rnorm(5)` | `rng.normal(0, 1, 5)` |
| Uniform draws | `runif(5)` | `rng.uniform(0, 1, 5)` |
| Sample | `sample(x, 3)` | `rng.choice(x, 3, replace=False)` |
| Matrix | `matrix(v, nrow=2)` | `np.array([[...],[...]])` |
| Dimensions | `dim(m)` | `m.shape` |
| Matrix indexing | `m[1, 2]` (1-based) | `m[0, 1]` (0-based) |



$\newline$



## 7. Pandas $\leftrightarrow$ dplyr

| Operation |  R (dplyr)  |    Pandas    |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Load package | `library(dplyr)` | `import pandas as pd` |
| Load CSV | `read_csv("file.csv")` | `pd.read_csv("file.csv")` |
| First rows | `head(df)` | `df.head()` |
| Dimensions | `dim(df)` | `df.shape` |
| Column types | `str(df)` | `df.info()` |
| Summary stats | `summary(df)` | `df.describe()` |
| Select column | `df$col` | `df["col"]` (Series) |
| Select columns | `select(df, a, b)` | `df[["a", "b"]]` (DataFrame) |
| Filter rows (Boolean indexing) | `df[df$col > val, ]` | `df[df["col"] > val]` |
| Filter rows | `filter(df, col > val)` | `df.query("col > val")` |
| Add/modify col | `df$new = expr` | `df["new"] = expr` |
| Add/modify col | `mutate(df, new = expr)` | `df.assign(new = expr)` |
| Sort | `arrange(df, col)` | `df.sort_values("col")` |
| Sort desc | `arrange(df, desc(col))` | `df.sort_values("col", ascending=False)` |
| Group + summarize | `group_by(df, col) |> summarize(m=mean(val))` | `df.groupby("col")["val"].mean()` |
| Multiple aggs | `summarize(m=mean(val), n=n())` | `df.agg(["mean","count"])` |
| Missing check | `is.na(df$col)` | `df["col"].isna()` |
| Drop missing | `na.omit(df)` | `df.dropna()` |
| Fill missing | `tidyr::replace_na(df, list(col=0))` | `df.fillna(0)` |
| Pipe chain | `df |> filter() |> group_by() |> summarize()` | `df.query().groupby().agg()` |
| Join on column | `left_join(df1, df2, by="key")` | `df1.merge(df2, on="key", how="left")` |
| Join on Index|    | `df1.join(df2, how="left")` |
| Wide → long | `pivot_longer(df, -id)` | `df.melt(id_vars="id")` |
| Long → wide | `pivot_wider(df, names_from=k, values_from=v)` | `df.pivot(index="id", columns="k", values="v")` |
| Factor | `factor(x, levels=..., ordered=TRUE)` | `pd.Categorical(x, categories=..., ordered=True)` |



$\newline$



## 8. Visualization

\vspace{0.5em}

### Matplotlib $\leftrightarrow$  Base R

| Concept | Base R | Matplotlib |
|---------|--------|-----------|
| Load package | *Part of base R* | `import matplotlib.pyplot as plt` |
| Figure + axes | `plot(x, y)` | `fig, ax = plt.subplots()` |
| Line plot | `plot(x, y, type="l")` | `ax.plot(x, y)` |
| Scatter | `plot(x, y)` | `ax.scatter(x, y)` |
| Bar chart | `barplot(heights)` | `ax.bar(labels, heights)` |
| Histogram | `hist(v)` | `ax.hist(v, bins=20)` |
| X label | `xlab("label")` | `ax.set_xlabel("label")` |
| Y label | `ylab("label")` | `ax.set_ylabel("label")` |
| Title | `main="title"` | `ax.set_title("title")` |
| Legend | `legend(...)` | `ax.legend()` |
| Show | *(automatic)* | `plt.show()` |

: {tbl-colwidths="[25,40,35]"}


\vspace{0.5em}


### Seaborn $\leftrightarrow$  ggplot2

Seaborn figure-level functions are the closest Python analog to ggplot2; they accept `data=`, `x=`, `y=`, `hue=`, `col=`, `row=`

| Task | ggplot2 | Seaborn |
|------|---------|---------|
| Load package | `library(ggplot2)` | `import seaborn as sns` |
| Distribution | `geom_histogram()` / `geom_density()` | `sns.displot(kind="hist"/"kde"/"ecdf")` |
| Scatter | `geom_point()` | `sns.relplot(kind="scatter")` |
| Line | `geom_line()` | `sns.relplot(kind="line")` |
| Bar | `geom_bar()` / `geom_col()` | `sns.catplot(kind="bar")` |
| Box plot | `geom_boxplot()` | `sns.catplot(kind="box")` |
| Violin | `geom_violin()` | `sns.catplot(kind="violin")` |
| Facet | `facet_wrap(~col)` | `col="col_name"` argument |
| Color by group | `aes(color=group)` | `hue="group"` argument |



$\newline$


## 9. Statistical Modeling

### Linear Regression

| Concept | R | Python |
|---------|---|--------|
| Load package | *Part of base R* | `import statsmodels.formula.api as smf` |
| Fit OLS | `lm(y ~ x, data=df)` | `smf.ols("y ~ x", data=df).fit()` |
| Summary | `summary(model)` | `model.summary()` |
| Coefficients | `coef(model)` | `model.params` |
| P-values | *(in summary)* | `model.pvalues` |
| Confidence intervals | `confint(model)` | `model.conf_int()` |
| R² | `summary(model)$r.squared` | `model.rsquared` |

: {tbl-colwidths="[25,35,40]"}


$\pagebreak$

### Distributions & Tests (scipy.stats)

With `from scipy import stats`, every distribution object has the same four operations:

| Goal | R | scipy.stats |
|------|---|-------------|
| Load package | *Part of base R* | `from scipy import stats` |
| Density | `dnorm(x)` | `stats.norm.pdf(x)` |
| CDF | `pnorm(x)` | `stats.norm.cdf(x)` |
| Quantile | `qnorm(p)` | `stats.norm.ppf(p)` |
| Random draws | `rnorm(n)` | `stats.norm.rvs(size=n)` |
| Two-sample t-test | `t.test(x, y)` | `stats.ttest_ind(x, y)` |

: {tbl-colwidths="[25,40,40]"}


The same pattern applies to `stats.t`, `stats.chi2`, `stats.binom`, and many more.


\vspace{0.5em}


### scikit-learn Workflow

```python
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression   # or any other model
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)

model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(X_train, y_train)          # train

y_pred = model.predict(X_test)       # predict
r2    = model.score(X_test, y_test)  # evaluate
rmse  = mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred)**0.5
```

The same three-step API (`fit`, `predict`, `score`) works for any sklearn estimator.



$\newline$



## 10. Key Python Pitfalls for R Users

| Pitfall | What R does | What Python does |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| `2^3` | `8` (exponentiation) | `1` (bitwise XOR!) — use `2**3` |
| `my_list + 1` | `c(1,2,3)+1` → `2,3,4` | `TypeError` — use NumPy |
| `b = a` (list) | Creates independent copy | Both point to the **same** list — use `a.copy()` |
| `TRUE`/`FALSE` | Case-insensitive | Must be `True`/`False` exactly |
| `NULL` / `NA` | Distinct concepts | `None` (no `NA` in base Python) |
| Implicit return | Last expression returned | Must use `return` explicitly |
| 1-based index | `v[1]` is first element | `my_list[0]` is first element |
| Slice endpoint | `v[1:3]` includes index 3 | `my_list[0:3]` excludes index 3 |
| `and` / `or` | `&&` / `\|\|` | `and` / `or` (symbols are bitwise) |
| Mutable default | Copy-on-modify protects you | `def f(x, my_list=[])` persists across calls — use `None` |
| `for x in obj:` | Works on any vector | Requires `__getitem__` (duck typing) |
